segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a vibrant montage of palm trees against a bright blue sky, accompanied by upbeat, dramatic reality TV-style music, setting a sunny, Californian tone. It quickly transitions to dynamic, fast-cut scenes featuring various individuals. A woman, identified by her voice as Becky, in a black hat and leather jacket, holds a small dog dressed in a pink outfit, declaring in a high-pitched, breathy voice, "I love champagne, kitties, and lipstick," followed by a cat meow. Another woman, Alexandria, in a leopard print dress, sits beside a man, Eugene, on a blue couch. Alexandria's confident voice states, "I love corn dogs, but there's nothing corny about me," while a dog barks. Eugene, wearing a red velvet tuxedo, speaks expressively to the camera, initially adopting a deep, gravelly, humorous persona, saying, "I like to steal things with my tiny human-like hands and I live for trash," before identifying himself with a raccoon chittering sound, "I'm raccoon." Laughter and various animal sounds, including an owl hoot and chicken cluck, punctuate these playful introductions. On-screen text introduces the show as 'The Real Housewives of Rank King,' parodying the popular reality series, with dramatic music building. The setting is a modern, well-lit living room with a marble coffee table, where small framed signs labeled 'BEST' and 'WORST' are placed in front of Becky and Alexandria. Eugene then formally welcomes viewers to "the Rank King Reality TV," introducing himself as "your Rank King, Eugene," and his companions as "my Rank Wife, Becky," and "my Rank Sister-Wife, Alexandria," with Becky and Alexandria playfully interjecting their names. He explains his role in deciding the "official final ranking," prompting Becky and Alexandria to sarcastically echo, "Patriarchy, am I right?" to which Eugene exasperatedly responds, "No, don't make it about that." The segment concludes with Eugene beginning to explain the challenge of ranking reality TV shows, maintaining the lighthearted, humorous, and exaggerated reality TV show atmosphere. A disclaimer notes the video was filmed in November 2020.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Eugene, dressed in a red velvet tuxedo, expressively explaining the show's premise from a modern living room couch, flanked by Becky, holding a small dog in a pink sweater, and Alexandria. He details how 32 reality TV shows across eight categories, each with at least ten seasons, will be ranked, as a title card visually reinforces the '32 SHOWS IN TOTAL EIGHT CATEGORIES' and specific categories like 'SINGING AND DANCING' and 'RICH PEOPLE'. Returning to the trio, Eugene outlines the judging criteria: "concept," "cast," and "how trashy is it," which is partially mirrored by a 'The Judging' title card over a golden glitter background. A comedic "whoosh" sound emphasizes his point about "trashy" shows. He then introduces the show's catchphrase, leading to a synchronized chant of "I'm right, you're wrong, shut up!" by Eugene, Becky, and Alexandria, visually accompanied by a 'PREVIOUSLY...' title card parodying 'The Real Housewives of Rank King' with the group pointing at each other. Eugene, Alexandria, and Becky each deliver their own versions of the catchphrase with distinct voices, punctuated by sound effects, with Eugene humorously critiquing Becky's "tone." The segment concludes with Eugene announcing "First round, singing and dancing competition shows," as a corresponding title card appears. A "whoa-whoa" sound effect precedes the display of an 'American Idol' clip on a screen, with Eugene placing a framed 'American Idol' sign on the table, and Alexandria immediately offering her opinion on its "original" concept.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a grand 'American Idol' stage, illuminated by spotlights and filled with an enthusiastic audience. The scene quickly cuts to a modern living room where Eugene, dressed in a red velvet tuxedo, Becky, holding her small dog in a pink sweater, and Alexandria, in a leopard print dress, are seated on a blue couch, engaging in a lively discussion. Framed 'BEST' and 'WORST' signs sit on the coffee table. Eugene, speaking expressively, critiques 'American Idol', stating what he doesn't love about the show, noting it was "on forever" and "they brought it back" after a farewell season, urging to "just let it die." Becky, smiling, interjects with "You're right." Eugene continues, acknowledging his love for guest hosts like "Nicki and Mariah and Katy," but finding the format "grown a little stale." He then praises "Ryan Seacrest," saying "he works his ass off." Becky, with a distinct female voice, suggests, "I think there's two of him," a thought echoed by Alexandria, who gestures animatedly and repeats, "I think there's two of him." Eugene questions, "You think there's two Ryan Seacrests?" to which Becky confirms, "Yeah," and Eugene adds, "There must be two." Eugene elaborates on Seacrest's omnipresence, being "on the radio every morning" and "a producer on Keeping Up with the Kardashians," as Alexandria hums "Mhm." Becky reiterates, "There's two Ryan Seacrests." Eugene then reveals his theory that "one of them is a merman," calling it "the perfect last name for a merman." He prompts them to "Think about it. Ryan Seacrest," as Alexandria responds "Uh-huh." Eugene continues, "He's a merman," with Alexandria again affirming "Mhm," describing the "amphibious one" who "comes out, does Kiss FM." Alexandria hums "Mhm" once more, and Becky agrees "Okay" to Eugene's suggestion to put the theory "in the middle for right now." The video alternates between the trio’s reactions and performance clips, showing an 'American Idol' contestant and then a group from 'The X Factor' under dramatic lighting. Eugene introduces, "So next we have The X Factor," noting 'American Idol' "has many iterations internationally and so does The X Factor." Alexandria adds, "Yeah. X Factor's how we got 1D." Eugene then mentions "Little Mix, one of my favorite pop groups," prompting Becky to ask, "You don't know who Little Mix is?" Eugene clarifies, "British." The segment concludes with a stylized title card reading 'The Judging' over a glittery background.

The scene then transitions to a modern living room where Eugene, Becky, and Alexandria are seated on a blue couch, engaging in a lively discussion. Eugene, in his red velvet tuxedo, gestures expressively as he speaks, noting his preference for the "cast drama" and internal tiffs among judges on 'The X Factor.' Becky, holding her small dog in a pink sweater, listens attentively and reacts with smiles, stating that 'American Idol' merely "cornered the market on like, stand there and sing," while 'The X Factor' is "making stars." On the coffee table, framed 'BEST' and 'WORST' signs are visible alongside small screens displaying logos for 'The X Factor,' 'American Idol,' and 'The Voice.' Eugene holds up the respective logos as he discusses them, introducing 'The Voice' and explaining its "blind auditions" concept. Becky elaborates, highlighting that if "more than one person hits the buzzer," the singer "gets to pick what judge they want to go with." Eugene then critiques 'The Voice,' stating it's "solely interesting because of the judges," a point Alexandria supports, adding that it's "not fun to watch after that" and hasn't "really helped anyone's career." A small dog's bark is heard briefly. Eugene then comments on the "trashiness" of these shows seeming "pretty low," before introducing a "fourth one" as a "great addition," to which Alexandria emphatically responds, "Burn it on fire. Just get 'em out." The segment includes clips from 'The Voice,' showing a contestant performing and a judge turning his chair, and then transitions to 'Dancing with the Stars,' with a clip of a female dancer in a light blue dress. The cozy, stylish interior, with soft lighting, a lamp, a wooden side table, and a kitchen in the background, maintains a casual yet animated atmosphere throughout their debate on the merits of various singing and dancing competition shows.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a vibrant montage of 'Dancing with the Stars' clips, showcasing energetic performances and judges' reactions, complete with the show's logo and 'CTV' branding. The scene then transitions to a modern living room where Eugene, dressed in a red velvet tuxedo, Becky, in a black hat and leather jacket holding her small dog in a pink sweater, and Alexandria, in a leopard print dress with her small dog in a red bandana, are seated on a blue couch. Eugene expressively discusses 'Dancing with the Stars,' remarking on its 'trash level' and 'stunt' casting, to which Becky interjects about 'Carol Baskin.' The trio continues their lively discussion, with Alexandria exclaiming about 'Sean Spicer' and declaring, 'That's not okay,' even threatening to 'light my TV on fire' if Trump appears next season, punctuated by a sound of fire. They then rank the show, with Becky placing it 'above The Voice' because she 'will watch a whole season of this,' while Alexandria agrees to 'pop it in there.' A title card with a golden bokeh background introduces 'Category Two: Shows About Real People.' Eugene then holds a framed 'The Real World' logo, introducing it as a 'classic.' Vintage clips from 'The Real World' appear, showing a 'Pedro Zamora SW 59th' street sign and an emotional man speaking, as Alexandria describes the premise of 'Eight strangers picked to live in a house.' Eugene recounts watching the early seasons, and Becky expresses her continued love for the show. Eugene further elaborates on the San Francisco season, highlighting Pedro, a cast member who was HIV positive, followed by a poignant voiceover from the clip stating, 'Pedro became a member of all of our families. Now no one in America can say they've never known.'

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Eugene, Becky, and Alexandria seated on a blue couch in a modern living room, discussing reality TV shows. Eugene, in a red velvet tuxedo, holds a 'THE REAL WORLD' sign and expressively speaks about the show's groundbreaking inclusion of "LGBT people" and a "very diverse cast racially," stating it "keeps it real." Becky, holding her small dog in a pink sweater, enthusiastically interjects, "I love Real World!" Eugene then transitions the discussion, placing the 'THE REAL WORLD' sign down and picking up a 'BIG BROTHER' sign, announcing it as "another show with real people under one roof." Alexandria, in a leopard print dress with her dog in a red bandana, reacts with an excited "Yes, yes, yes, yes, gettin' lit!" and elaborates, describing 'Big Brother' as the show for "conniving people" and "sneaky" competition. A clip from 'Big Brother' then shows a contestant with a transparent object on their head and a man with a serious expression, with a voiceover stating, "I have to get them to despise me." Returning to the trio, Eugene speculates on the show's casting, suggesting they might be "casting for that one magical villain" or "too much of those bad people." He then suggests ranking 'Big Brother' "below Real World," a sentiment Alexandria echoes. Eugene then holds up a 'SHARK TANK' sign, announcing the next show. A 'Shark Tank' clip features a bald man gesturing and a woman on a stool, while Eugene describes the show's "entrepreneurs" and "investors," naming "Mark Cuban, the bald guy, the blonde lady, the other dude, the guru guy." As Alexandria points and gestures animatedly, Eugene explains that "everyday people come and pitch their products." Becky adds that they "get real things from it," citing "Squatty Potty," which surprises Eugene. The segment concludes with Eugene holding the 'SHARK TANK' sign, looking at the camera, as Becky and Alexandria remain engaged, with a "Shit" sound effect and a voice saying "Literally" closing out the minute.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Eugene, Becky, and Alexandria seated on a blue couch in a modern living room, engaging in a lively discussion about reality TV shows. Eugene, dressed in a red velvet tuxedo, holds up a framed sign reading 'SHARK TANK' as he asks, "Where would you put this?" Alexandria, in a leopard print dress and holding her small dog, responds, "I'd put it second," while Becky, wearing a black hat and leather jacket and holding her small dog, adds, "I would agree, I would like to keep Real World at the top." Eugene then transitions to a segment titled 'SHOWS ABOUT REAL PEOPLE' with a "Boom!" and announces "Jersey Shore," holding up a 'JERSEY SHORE' sign. Alexandria and Becky chime in, calling it "The realest of the real." Alexandria excitedly exclaims, "Oh, my board game!" as she picks up the 'Jersey Shore Trivia Game' box, revealing she "watched all ten seasons" when unemployed and can now "play this trivia game anytime and I can win." Becky then recalls, "I still think the most like iconic scene of reality TV is Snooki reading that letter that she wrote," prompting Alexandria to ask about "Ronnie like rubbing his face in someone's breast?" and Becky to quote, "'Dear Sammy, the other night at bed...'" A clip from 'Jersey Shore' then plays, showing a woman at a table with the infamous note, accompanied by a voiceover stating, "Put his head in between a cocktail waitress's breast." Returning to the trio, they continue laughing, with Becky reiterating, "Ronnie had his face in between two cocktail waitresses' breasts!" and Alexandria dramatically adding, "And she read it so seriously!" Eugene comments, "Y'all, the trash level on this show is through the roof." Another 'Jersey Shore' clip shows a group of people being escorted by police, with a text overlay identifying 'JENNI "JWOWW"', and a voiceover saying, "Being a nuisance, like, really? I didn't even know there was a charge for that," followed by a quick "Boing!" sound effect. The segment ends with Eugene speaking directly to the camera, maintaining the energetic and humorous tone of the discussion.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a golden bokeh background and the text 'Category Three Cooking Shows,' accompanied by upbeat, slightly dramatic music. The scene transitions to a modern living room where Eugene, Becky, and Alexandria are seated on a blue couch with their small dogs. Eugene, in a red velvet tuxedo, holds a 'Top Chef' sign as Alexandria exclaims, 'Good person,' and Becky adds, 'It's the best though.' Eugene interjects, 'Jersey Shore sec,' to which Alexandria responds, 'Oh, that's fair,' as they react expressively. Eugene then introduces 'cooking shows' and 'Top Chef,' describing it as 'a pure competition show where the talent and the contestants are serious about it,' while Alexandria points and gestures animatedly, explaining, 'You're rooting for everyone. You're not trying to like get the trash from here.' Eugene adds, 'But I also love Padma's Jonathan.' A clip from 'Top Chef' shows a chef preparing a dish, accompanied by a dramatic, slightly melancholic voiceover stating, 'Please pack your knives and go,' which is then repeated, 'Pack your knives and go,' as the scene cuts to a close-up of Alexandria reacting. The scene returns to the trio, with Eugene now holding a 'Hell's Kitchen' sign. He announces, 'Okay, now let's go on the opposite end of uh nice hosts. Hell's Kitchen,' as the music shifts to a more intense, dramatic tone, punctuated by a 'Hey chef!' sound effect. Eugene continues, 'Or even Tom Colicchio. Let's talk about Gordon, the star.' Becky enthusiastically declares, 'I love Gordon,' and Eugene confirms, 'Gordon Ramsay.' A split-screen appears, showing Gordon Ramsay on the left and a TikTok user eating pizza on the right, with '@gordonramsayofficial' visible. Alexandria comments, 'He looks like a giant thumb,' and Becky adds, 'Like remember the thumb-thums from Spy Kids? He looks just like that. But I also love him,' as the trio laughs. Eugene suggests, 'And he is someone who I feel like is mean for cameras.' Alexandria counters, 'He's also like a really good family man, like he's always posting his kids and supporting them,' and Becky adds, 'He roasts people on TikTok.' Alexandria finds it 'very cute.' Eugene notes, 'He's very up with the social media,' and Becky adds, 'He's up with the kids. Also he gave us the iconic...' Eugene then prompts, 'Ready, Eugene?' as Becky playfully adjusts his bow tie. The segment concludes with the trio laughing and engaging, as iconic Gordon Ramsay voiceovers exclaim, 'What are you!', 'What are you!', and 'An idiot sandwich!', followed by a sound effect of 'I'm an idiot sandwich!', reinforcing the humorous reality TV parody theme with 'BEST' and 'WORST' signs visible on the coffee table.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Eugene, dressed in a red velvet tuxedo with a black bow tie, Becky in a black hat and leather jacket holding her small dog in a pink sweater, and Alexandria in a leopard print dress holding her small dog with a red bandana, seated on a blue couch. Eugene asks if a show is "above Top Chef," to which Alexandria, holding her small dog, responds, "For me, it is. Yeah." Becky, holding her small dog, states her opinion that "Hell's Kitchen is gonna go lower than Top Chef because I enjoy Kitchen Nightmares more," as Eugene holds up a framed sign for 'Hell's Kitchen' and its title card appears. Eugene, gesturing expressively, asserts that "This is based on star power alone. Gordon Ramsay puts this above Top Chef," prompting Alexandria to exclaim, "Whoa." Eugene then announces the "Next show, Chopped," holding up the 'CHOPPED' sign as Alexandria smiles and points, and Becky’s dog looks around curiously. A clip of 'Chopped' featuring a contestant in a blue-lit kitchen is shown as Eugene details, "Chopped concept is incredible. It's basically you bring in four amateur chefs, you give them a basket with one ingredient," adding examples like "rice, you got chicken, and Ranchers." Becky expresses her affection for the show, saying, "I love this show. I love that like there's always a little bit of drama." Eugene then categorizes "Food Network and HGTV and Discovery" as "hotel shows" due to their lack of a "stand-up cast," placing them "at the bottom." Finally, Eugene declares, "All right, and the final cooking show, The Great British Bake Off," holding up its sign. Alexandria laughs and agrees, "Yeah, number one. Thank you, thank you," as Eugene praises it as "Number one" and challenges, "How are you gonna argue that? Do you watch this?" Becky admits, "I don't hate it. I just never got into it. But I do miss the trash. I need the trash." As a clip of 'The Great British Bake Off' plays, featuring a contestant with a fox mask and judges, Eugene praises its "Concept, great. Cast, amazing," describing it as "the opposite of trash, but it's on the same spectrum," and concludes by calling it "Twee." The atmosphere is playful and humorous, with the hosts reacting to each show with animated expressions and gestures.

Eugene asks "What is tween?" and Becky echoes "Yeah, what do you tween?", prompting Eugene to explain it as "British kawaii... trash but with tween" that becomes "like an addiction." As the category 'COOKING SHOWS' appears on screen, Eugene holds up a 'The Great British Bake Off' sign. Becky, holding her small dog in a pink sweater, and Alexandria, with her dog in a red bandana, react with amusement as the dogs become playful. Eugene asks "Do you like Great British Bake Off?" and Becky repeats the question. Alexandria points to the 'WORST' sign on the coffee table, indicating her opinion, while Eugene, gesturing expressively, asks what will be made for a "showstopper." In a dramatic, deep voice, Eugene declares he's going to make "soggy, soggy bottom biscuits," which Becky and Alexandria both emphatically reject with "No, soggy bottom." Becky then states "Number one. I think I need to watch it more," and Alexandria adds "This sounds great." Eugene enthusiastically proclaims "Bake Off! Number one food show in our book!" The trio laughs as Becky asks "Where'd he go?", and Eugene explains, "He was like, 'I'm not part of this parody anymore.'" A magical transition sound and golden bokeh effect lead to a new, more dramatic background music as 'Category Four: Shows About Rich People' appears. Eugene announces, "The next category... These are shows about rich people," to excited "Yay!" cheers from Becky and Alexandria. Eugene adds, "Not my personal category, so I'm gonna let them fight it out," before introducing "the iconic Real Housewives!" to more enthusiastic "Woo!" and "Yeah!" cheers. As Alexandria points to the screen showing a clip of 'ADRIENNE' from #RHOBH, Becky playfully adjusts Eugene’s bow tie, and she begins to explain, "It's rich women, you don't even have to be a housewife, you don't have to be..." before the segment ends, maintaining a fun and energetic atmosphere with the dogs adding to the humor.

The scene then cuts to Eugene, dressed in a red velvet tuxedo with a black bow tie, seated on a blue couch, holding a framed 'Survivor' sign and gesturing expressively as he introduces the show. He comments on the gameplay, stating, "The gameplay, you can't, you can't, you can't knock it," with Becky interjecting, "You can't be that." Clips from 'Survivor' show a tribal council and a contestant celebrating in a jungle. Back in the studio, Eugene places the 'Survivor' sign on the coffee table between 'BEST' and 'WORST' signs, while Becky, in a black hat and leather jacket, holds her small dog in a pink sweater, and Alexandria, in a leopard print dress, holds her dog with a red bandana. He muses, "When it comes to cast, I can't even remember the name of the host," prompting Becky to ask, "There's no host?" Finally, Eugene introduces 'The Challenge', holding its sign, stating, "Now the third show we have in the adventure category is The Challenge," which "once was called Real World Road Rules Challenge." Becky excitedly interjects "Challenge!" and Alexandria repeats "Real World Road Rules!" as clips show a contestant hanging from a helicopter, a man in a blue shirt, and contestants racing on a wet surface, accompanied by upbeat, energetic music and cheering. Eugene concludes by noting 'The Challenge' is "one of the longest reality shows ever." The modern living room setting with bright lighting remains consistent throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an intense clip from 'The Challenge,' featuring contestants on a physical obstacle course with a timer at '00:08,' accompanied by dramatic music and whooshing sounds. A male voice, later identified as Eugene, questions, "I think younger people are watching this. Because you still watch MTV shows, is the question." The scene then cuts to Eugene, dressed in a red velvet tuxedo, Becky in a black hat and leather jacket holding her small dog, and Alexandria in a leopard print dress holding her small dog, all seated on a blue couch. Eugene holds up a 'The Challenge' sign, while Becky laughs and states, "But I don't think that..." with Alexandria adding, "Yeah." Eugene then announces, "And the last adventure show we have... Naked and Afraid," holding up a corresponding sign. Becky reacts with surprise and strong dislike, exclaiming, "I hate it. My dad, it's a dad show. My dad'll put it on and I'll just leave it on for hours." She continues, "And I'm like, these people are naked but they are not afraid. Nobody's gonna die. They're not gonna let you die. You're being filmed. What are you gonna starve to death and Jonathan's gonna be like zooming in?" A clip from 'Naked and Afraid' shows a man and woman covered in mud in a jungle, during which a deep, resonant male voice-over emphatically states, "No." Returning to the trio, Becky reiterates, "No. They're not afraid. The show is a lie. Put it at the bottom! I hate it!" Eugene then suggests, "So this should be Naked and..." to which Becky quickly interjects, "Naked and confused," prompting laughter from all three. Becky further elaborates on her disappointment, stating, "And they're not actually naked. Like we don't see anyone's like these penises or vaginas." She humorously demands, "If it was really Naked and Afraid, I want to see someone's like dick floiling," to which Eugene interjects, "I don't want to see that." Becky continues, "Slapping both sides of their legs," and then, as Eugene mentions "Hunger Games," she adds, "I want to see women running with their boobs flopping them in the head." The segment intersperses more clips from 'Naked and Afraid,' including a woman in a red bandana walking through a rocky area, while the trio continues laughing and discussing the show, with Becky concluding, "But I hate when they go to like tropical climates and they're..." The on-screen text 'ADVENTURE SHOWS NAKED AND AFRAID' and the Discovery Channel logo are visible during the clips.

Alexandria, holding her small dog, passionately states, "I don't really want to see 30 women fighting for one man, but 30 men fighting for one woman sounds good." Eugene, in a red velvet tuxedo, then introduces the topic, explaining they've "divided them up into two different shows" as he holds up a framed 'BACHELOR' sign. He asks, "Which one's better?" Becky, holding her small dog in a pink sweater, quickly responds, "Bachelorette." Alexandria elaborates, arguing 'The Bachelor' has "trashier drama" but 'The Bachelorette' has "women calling the shots" and "way more successful couples," citing "Chad" and "Luke P." as examples of "horrible people" from 'The Bachelor' as a clip from the show plays, featuring a male contestant's dialogue. Eugene dismisses the clip as "a pile of baloney" and reiterates his preference for "Bachelorette," calling it a "better show." The video transitions to a 'The Bachelorette' clip featuring a proposal and a surprised woman, followed by a dark outdoor shot and a concerned woman in leopard print. Back on the couch, Eugene holds both 'BACHELOR' and 'BACHELORETTE' signs before introducing "Another show, Teen Mom," holding up a 'TEEN MOM' sign. He asks if they watch it, and Becky confirms "Almost all of it," with Alexandria adding she "watched a lot of it." Alexandria then provides a detailed analysis of 'Teen Mom,' describing it as "really real" with "no fake activities," where "producers are there to almost more support these people." segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Eugene, Becky, and Alexandria seated on a blue couch in a modern, well-lit living room, continuing their lively discussion on reality TV. Eugene, in his red velvet tuxedo, transitions from the 'Teen Mom' topic, stating in a clear male voice that while he didn't watch much, the few episodes he saw "made me sad" and it's "certainly not trash entertainment." He then introduces "Married at First Sight," holding up a framed sign for the show. Alexandria, in her leopard print dress, animatedly expresses her love for the show in a clear female voice, asking "Who's the lucky lady?" Eugene replies he doesn't know yet. As the video cuts to clips from 'Married at First Sight' showing a woman eating and a distressed bald man, Alexandria explains the show's premise: "Thousands of people apply for this show and they really are trying to find like your perfect match. You're legally getting married to them on the first episode." She continues, describing how contestants see each other at the altar for the first time, leading to "true reaction," with Eugene interjecting an "Oh!" and sounds of agreement. Alexandria notes that "some people you could tell like they think the other person is unattractive right away," and it's "really sad when you could tell they're disappointed." Becky, holding her small dog in a pink sweater, chimes in with a distinct female voice, asking, "I don't know if my husband is attracted to me." segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Eugene, Becky, and Alexandria seated on a blue couch in a modern living room, engaging in a lively discussion about reality TV shows. Eugene, dressed in a red velvet tuxedo with a black bow tie, holds a framed sign labeled 'MARRIED AT FIRST SIGHT' and expressively states, "I guess we're gonna put Married at First Sight right here." He then proposes his own show concept, "I want a show called The Divorcé," explaining that it involves "a couple getting divorced, but to actually decide who gets what, like I want the dog. Laughter erupts, and Becky, wearing a black hat and leather jacket and holding her small dog in a pink sweater, chimes in, "It's like the gay house," to which Eugene responds, "And this spin-off is called The Gay Divorcé." Alexandria, in a leopard print dress, holds her small dog with a red bandana, smiling and reacting. The coffee table in front of them displays 'BEST' and 'WORST' signs. A whooshing sound accompanies a transition to a title card reading 'EUGENE LEE YANG PRESENTS THE DIVORCEE' over a church steeple, followed by another for 'Category Seven: Style & Transformation', which Eugene introduces vocally as "Style and Transformation shows." The trio returns, with Eugene now holding a sign for 'AMERICA'S NEXT TOP MODEL', as he enthusiastically declares, "You wanna be on top!" and introduces "America's Next Top Model, very simple concept. You're trying to find the top model, Tyra is the host. This show is so entertaining." Clips from 'America's Next Top Model' are displayed, featuring a blonde woman, a young girl in a white dress, a contestant in a blue dress, a woman in a black top, and a young girl in a yellow dress, accompanied by dramatic show audio like "America's Next Top Model is... Hello! He's crazy to say that there's no room for your girls in the industry!" and a judge's voice stating, "And Noel, we're making you into a traditionally African woman with a head wrap." The group reacts with amusement and surprise, with Becky covering her mouth and Alexandria pointing animatedly, while Eugene offers critical commentary, noting, "There are a lot of problematic things when you look back at it. Certainly some of the shoots were not really well thought out," and observing that "Tyra became sort of like this almost like parody of herself." The atmosphere remains playful and humorous, with animated conversation and laughter.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a clip from 'Flavor of Love,' showing a woman in a white dress embraced by a man in a dimly lit room, followed by a group dancing in a club with purple lighting. Eugene, dressed in a red velvet tuxedo, then appears holding a framed 'FLAVOR OF LOVE' sign, speaking animatedly about "the flave, the contestants, and then also the casting and producing team knew that going in." He is joined by Becky, in a black hat and leather jacket, holding her small dog in a pink sweater, and Alexandria, in a leopard print dress, holding her small dog with a red bandana, as they sit on a blue couch with 'BEST' and 'WORST' signs on the coffee table. Becky asks, "Do you remember Paris Hilton's show like where she had a competition?" to which Alexandria interjects, "To be her best friend. To be her best friend. Oh yeah!" Becky continues, noting, "I feel like this is in the same vein where like they know they're being made fun of, they're playing into it. Everyone know there knows it like it's kind of a joke." Eugene then asks, "It's the next one mine?" and Becky confirms, "Yes." Becky further inquires, "How much of a song can you sing on camera before you have to pay for the song?" to which Eugene quickly replies, "Less than five seconds." Alexandria then dramatically sings, "You know why I love Vanderpump Rules? 'Cause I love watching people who are worse than me," followed by a car horn sound. The video transitions to a 'Vanderpump Rules' clip, showing a man pouring a drink into a martini glass and a woman in a purple dress walking confidently, as the trio reacts with Becky gesturing and Alexandria smiling. A title card 'RANK TEAM PICKS VANDERPUMP RULES' appears while Eugene holds up a 'VANDERPUMP RULES' sign. Alexandria declares, "This show is full of bad people," with Becky adding, "They're hot," and Alexandria agreeing, "They're hot." Eugene makes a "woo" sound, then says, "Some of them are hot," but Alexandria asserts, "But they are very irredeemable." More 'Vanderpump Rules' clips show emotional moments, including a distressed woman in white and a concerned woman in black. Becky asks, "Except for who?" and Eugene names, "Brittany." Intense, dramatic music plays as Alexandria exclaims, "No!" and Eugene makes a sound of disbelief. Becky states, "I don't think she's a great person," while Alexandria passionately shouts, "Rot in hell! Rot in hell!" Eugene adds, "He started rot in hell." The trio continues their commentary, with Becky petting her dog and Alexandria laughing, as Eugene explains, "So they used to be like these servers and they were all like just whatever shitty thing..." The segment concludes with a close-up of a man in a black shirt looking serious.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Eugene, Becky, and Alexandria seated on a blue couch, engaging in a lively discussion about reality TV shows. Eugene, in a red velvet tuxedo, holds a framed 'VANDERPUMP RULES' sign, while Becky, in a black hat, holds her small dog in a pink sweater, and Alexandria, in a leopard print dress, holds her small dog with a red bandana. 'BEST' and 'WORST' signs are visible on the coffee table. Alexandria's voice initiates the conversation, humorously stating, "They did it. They did it," referring to someone impregnating another in Vegas, and adding, "before I take like a thousand more shots, just to clear my mind." Becky's voice then asks, "Do you take birth control?" to which Alexandria replies, "Mm-mm." Alexandria continues, revealing, "They're all pregnant at the same time. It's like they did a pregnancy pact 'cause a couple of them got fired 'cause they're like super racist and problematic," concluding that it's "a good show to watch if you ever want to be like, 'Well, I'm not that bad.'" Eugene, gesturing expressively, interjects, "I know you love Vandy. Do you think Vanderpump is better than Flavor of Love?" The scene cuts to a 'Vanderpump Rules' clip showing a woman in a pink dress, a distressed woman, and a Variety news headline about Stassi Schroeder and Kristen Doute being fired for racist actions. Becky affirms, "Oh yeah," as Alexandria praises the show, "It's such a good show. Like I'm so like I still kind of care about what they're doing." Eugene adds, "It's still going on." Alexandria then humorously labels the show as "quintessential like trash," followed by a brief squealing sound. Eugene comments on "Your show selection," as Becky enthusiastically declares, "I love this show." The segment transitions with Alexandria holding a framed 'The Girls Next Door' sign. Becky passionately states, "The Girls Next Door is the best reality show to ever be on TV. This is kind of wealth porn too," elaborating that it "followed Hugh Hefner's three girlfriends at the time." The scene transitions to a clip from 'The Girls Next Door,' showing a woman in a white top, a man in a black shirt, and a woman in a black crop top dancing. Becky describes it as "one of the first reality shows where like it was wholesome if that makes sense. Like families watch this together because like one of the girls was like an athlete and one of the other girls had a brother who was in the army." The trio reacts with laughter and commentary as Eugene humorously remarks, "Again, when I see so many blonde people, I like slowly my eyes cross."

Eugene defends his choice, asking, "You know how many blonde people are on this show?" Becky and Alexandria retort, "Oh, they're here! It's the perfect show!" Alexandria places it, declaring, "We outnumber you. This is democracy." Eugene questions, "Is it?" and Alexandria confirms, "Yeah, we won the popular vote and the electoral college." Becky chimes in, "There's so many seasons, it's one of the most successful shows ever." Eugene then speaks directly to the camera, with on-screen text appearing, stating, "I am not happy about this, this is my professional. I am not happy about what just happened. Did you see what just happened?" He then holds up a 'SURVIVOR' sign. Becky laughs and says, "No!" while Alexandria states, "I don't care about it." Eugene argues, "Survivor is iconic. I would honestly put Survivor above Housewives." Both Becky and Alexandria exclaim, "No!" Eugene then says, "Okay, it sounds like y'all have a..." before Alexandria interjects, "Below, below Real World," with Becky agreeing, "Below Real World." Eugene concludes, "Okay, you're not here. I'm gonna let them rank," as on-screen text appears: 'EUGENE Not happy about all the RICH BLONDE PEOPLE', followed by 'EUGENE THIS ISN'T A RANK-OCRACY, IT'S A RANK-TATORSHIP'.
